hero.title: The Spencers and the Evans
hero.subtitle: Five generations between Clerkenwell and Islington — costermongers, printers, and the Blitz

media.alt: St John's Gate, Clerkenwell, photographed by Henry Dixon in 1880 — the medieval gateway to the Priory of St John of Jerusalem
media.caption: St John's Gate, Clerkenwell, 1880. Photograph by Henry Dixon. The gate stood at the heart of the neighbourhood where the Spencer family raised seven children

media.src: media/docs/St Thomas Charterhouse marriage register 1865 pages 18-19 Norman French Spencer Evans.jpg
media.alt: Parish register from St Thomas Charterhouse, Finsbury, showing marriage entries from 1865 — the year Edward Spencer married Eliza Thomas
media.caption: St Thomas Charterhouse marriage register, 1865 — the year Edward Spencer married Eliza Thomas. The church was demolished in 1909

media.alt: Ancestry record showing the birth and christening of Louisa Spencer at Saint Thomas Charterhouse, Finsbury, 1871
media.caption: Louisa Spencer's christening record — baptised 16 July 1871 at Saint Thomas Charterhouse, Finsbury. Her future husband's family, the Fensoms, were raising children in Islington a mile to the north

media.alt: Photograph captioned as William Evans and Louisa Spencer — the couple who married in 1891 and connected Clerkenwell to the Evans line
media.caption: William Evans and Louisa Spencer — married 26 July 1891. William's origins remain unknown; Evans is overwhelmingly a Welsh surname

media.alt: A London costermonger with his barrow, photographed by John Thomson for Street Life in London, 1877
media.caption: A London costermonger, 1877. Photograph by John Thomson. Alfred Evans was listed as a Costermonger in the 1939 Register — a street seller from a barrow, one of the oldest London trades

media.alt: Portrait of Alfred Edward William Evans — born Holborn 1893, christened at Holy Redeemer Exmouth Market, costermonger
media.caption: Alfred Edward William Evans — born 2 February 1893, christened at Holy Redeemer, Exmouth Market. He married Annie Fensom on Christmas Day 1912

media.alt: Bomb damage in London during the Blitz, 1940 — Holborn suffered the highest rate of damage per capita in the country
media.caption: London Blitz, 1940. Joseph Evans was born on 28 August 1940 — ten days before the bombing began. Holborn suffered the highest bomb damage per capita in the entire country

media.alt: Walker House, Ossulston Estate, Somers Town — the Grade II listed council estate where the next generation of the Evans family settled
media.caption: Walker House, Ossulston Estate, Somers Town. Built 1929–37 by the LCC to replace slums. Helia Cerpa-Pérez settled here after exile from Chile

media.alt: Second portrait of Alfred Edward William Evans — the costermonger whose family spanned five generations in the same few London streets
media.caption: Alfred Edward William Evans. From Clerkenwell to Islington to Camden, the family moved barely two miles in five generations
